fact: January 18 is the 18th day of the year, which means there are exactly 347 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is smack dab in the middle of winter's deepest freeze - the coldest temperatures typically happen right around now!
Down in Antarctica, January 18 falls during the height of summer when penguins are busy feeding their fluffy gray chicks.
The sun rises super late and sets super early in northern places like Alaska - some towns only get about 5 hours of daylight!
Tree sap is completely frozen solid in cold regions, making trees crack and pop with loud snapping sounds on really frigid nights.
January 18 falls under the zodiac sign Capricorn, represented by a half-goat, half-fish creature that can climb mountains and swim in oceans.
Polar bears in the Arctic are at their fluffiest right now - their winter coats are so thick that snow doesn't even melt when it lands on them!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, this date falls during scorching summer when cicadas buzz so loudly they can drown out conversations.
The Earth is actually closest to the sun around this time of year, even though it's freezing cold in many places - weird, right?
Owls are extra active on January nights because small animals leave more tracks in the snow, making them easier to hunt.
In northern regions, the air is so cold and dry that your breath creates instant fog clouds that can freeze into tiny ice crystals.
